prompt:
Ticket for the platform team: incidentctl, the incident-management CLI for our on-prem fleet. Pure python3 stdlib, no daemons, no database — one JSON state file, driven entirely by subcommands, so it works over ssh on every box we have. I've written the acceptance suite (test_incidentctl.py, run `python3 test_incidentctl.py`, don't edit it); this ticket is the spec.

Ground rules. NOTHING in the package may read the wall clock — bit us during last quarter's audit replay. The entry point is `incidents.cli.main(argv, *, now, state_path) -> int`: argv is the subcommand + args, `now` is the invocation's idea of current time as 'YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM' (the bin wrapper passes the real clock in prod; tests and replays inject), state_path is the JSON state file. main returns the exit code (never calls sys.exit) and prints to stdout/stderr. All timestamps everywhere are naive 'YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM' strings, minute math throughout. Package layout is fixed — split it as incidents/__init__.py, store.py (state load/save), ingest.py (line parsing + validation), grouping.py (fingerprint/window rules), rotation.py (schedule math), escalation.py (policies, pages, tick), timeline.py (export), cli.py (argv dispatch + output). Keep the logic in the domain modules; cli.py should just parse and delegate.

State: one JSON document. Missing file = fresh empty state; file that doesn't parse as a JSON object = print 'error: state file is corrupt: <path>' to stderr and exit 2, for every command. Mutating commands persist only when they succeed (exit 0) — a failed command must leave the file byte-for-byte alone. Read-only commands (oncall, handoffs, list, timeline) never write. Errors print one line to stderr, 'error: <message>', and exit 1 for domain errors (unknown rotation/policy/incident, duplicates, wrong state) or 2 for usage problems (unknown command 'error: unknown command: <name>', missing/malformed arguments, bad timestamp arguments, unreadable files). Success output is exactly what's specified below, nothing more.

Rotations. `rotation add NAME --people a,b,c --start T --shift-hours H` (order of people matters; duplicate name → 'error: rotation NAME already exists'; empty people list, H < 1, bad T → exit 2). Prints 'added rotation NAME'. The person on call at time T is people[((T - start) in minutes) // (H*60) % len(people)] for T >= start; strictly before start it's the literal 'nobody'. Shift boundaries belong to the incoming person. `override add ROTATION PERSON --from T --until U` (half-open [from, until), from < until required, unknown rotation is a domain error) prints 'added override on ROTATION'; when overrides overlap, the most recently added one covering T wins; the person needn't be a rotation member. `oncall ROTATION [--at T]` prints just the name (default T = now). `handoffs ROTATION --count N [--at T]' prints the next N base-schedule boundaries strictly after T, one per line, '<time> <incoming person>' — the rotation start itself is the first boundary; overrides are deliberately ignored here; N < 1 is a usage error.

Escalation policies. `policy add NAME --step TARGET@MINUTES ... --step TARGET` — an ordered ladder. TARGET is user:<name> or rotation:<name> (rotation must already exist → domain error otherwise). Every step except the last carries @MINUTES (positive int): how long after THIS page fires before the next level fires if nobody has acked. Missing @ on a non-last step, non-integer minutes, or a malformed target → exit 2. Duplicate policy name → domain error. Prints 'added policy NAME'. `route add SERVICE POLICY` maps a service to a policy (unknown policy → domain error, re-routing overwrites silently), prints 'routed SERVICE to POLICY'. Services without a route open incidents normally but never page.

Ingest. `ingest FILE [--strict] [--window N]` reads JSON-lines alerts: {"at", "service", "check", "severity", "message"}, severity one of low|high|critical, unknown extra keys ignored, blank/whitespace lines skipped silently. Missing FILE → 'error: alerts file not found: <path>' exit 2. Validation reasons, exactly these strings, checked in this order per line: 'invalid json' (unparseable), 'not an object', then per field in the order at, service, check, severity, message: "missing field '<f>'" then "wrong type for field '<f>'" (must be strings); then "bad timestamp '<raw>'" (strict YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM), then "empty field 'service'"/"empty field 'check'", then "bad severity '<raw>'". Default mode: report each bad line to stderr as 'line <n>: <reason>' (1-based physical line number), ingest the good ones, exit 0. --strict: the FIRST bad line prints its 'line <n>: <reason>' and aborts with exit 1, state untouched, nothing ingested. Summary on stdout (always, default mode): 'ingested A alert(s): N new, G grouped, R rejected' where A = accepted count.

Grouping: fingerprint = 'service/check'. Alerts are processed in file order. An alert joins the open (unresolved) incident with the same fingerprint that has the newest last-seen time, iff (alert.at - last_seen) <= window minutes (window default 15, --window N overrides for that invocation, N >= 1). Late/out-of-order alerts (at < last_seen) therefore always join that incident; last-seen never moves backwards. No candidate or gap too big → a NEW incident: id 'INC-<n>' (global counter from 1), opened_at = the alert's at. An incident's severity is the max seen (low < high < critical); resolved incidents never accept alerts.

Paging: when an incident opens for a routed service, level 0 of its policy fires immediately at opened_at — recorded in the incident, NOT printed by ingest (prod tails pages from state; ingest stdout stays parseable). A rotation:<r> target resolves to whoever is on call at the page's due time (possibly 'nobody'); user:<u> is literal. Level i > 0 falls due at opened_at + sum of the @MINUTES of levels 0..i-1. `tick` materializes pages: across every incident, fire every not-yet-fired level with due <= now, in (due, incident id) order, printing 'page <id> level <i> -> <target> (<due>)' per page — rotation targets resolve at the DUE time, and the page is recorded with its due time, not now. An ack or resolve at time X cuts the ladder: levels with due >= X are suppressed forever, levels with due < X still fire on a later tick. Nothing due → print exactly 'no pages due'. Tick must be idempotent.

Lifecycle: `ack INC-n --by PERSON` (open, un-acked; errors 'error: unknown incident: <id>', 'error: <id> already acked', 'error: <id> is resolved') prints 'acked <id>' and records the ack at now. `resolve INC-n` (open; same unknown/resolved errors) prints 'resolved <id>', records at now. Acked incidents are still open until resolved.

Reporting: `list [--all]` prints one line per incident in id order: '<id> <status> <severity> <fingerprint> alerts=<count> last=<last_seen>' where status is open|acked|resolved; default hides resolved, --all shows everything; no matches → 'no incidents'. `timeline INC-n [--format text|json]` merges the incident's alerts, pages, ack, resolve into one stream ordered by time, ties broken alert < page < ack < resolve, then insertion order. text (default): header 'timeline <id> (<fingerprint>)', then '<at> alert <severity> <message>', '<at> page level=<i> <target>', '<at> ack <person>', '<at> resolve'. json: one JSON object {"incident", "fingerprint", "status", "events": [...]} with event objects {"at","kind":"alert","severity","message"} / {"at","kind":"page","level","target"} / {"at","kind":"ack","by"} / {"at","kind":"resolve"} — exactly those keys.

That's the whole surface. Get `python3 test_incidentctl.py` green without touching the test file.
